critic_raw:
The multimodal neural network for post-operative complication prediction makes the same fundamental comparison mistake as many multi-modal versus logistic regression designs: the neural network is trained on both structured clinical features and unstructured clinical text, while the logistic regression baseline uses only structured features. The design explicitly states 'the baseline does not incorporate text embeddings' and frames this as isolating 'the improvement from multimodal integration and neural capacity.' But this conflates two distinct hypotheses — is multimodal information useful? and is neural capacity useful? — into a single uninterpretable comparison. A single-modality neural network or a text-only logistic regression would be necessary to decompose these contributions.

The primary evaluation metric is sensitivity at a fixed specificity of 85%. While clinically motivated, this choice introduces sensitivity to the decision threshold selected on the validation set. Sensitivity at 85% specificity measured on the validation set will differ from the same threshold applied to the test set due to distributional differences between the two periods. The design does not describe how the threshold is transferred from validation to test, whether the 85% specificity is enforced on the test set directly (threshold recalibration, which is a form of test set touch) or whether the validation-selected threshold is applied without adjustment. This ambiguity could result in either test set snooping or substantial threshold mismatch.

The stratification within temporal windows by surgeon and complication rate quartile is a sound design decision, but the surgeon stratification introduces a subtle multiple testing concern: with many surgeons, ensuring each is proportionally represented across splits may produce very small per-surgeon cells, making the stratification impractical or unstable. Moreover, the design includes surgeon identifier as a fixed effect feature, but in a temporal split, surgeons present in training may retire or join the system during later periods, causing identifier missingness or category drift that is not addressed.
defender_raw:
The multimodal neural network for post-operative complication prediction is designed around the right question: does adding unstructured operative and pre-operative text to structured clinical features improve complication detection beyond what structured data alone can achieve? The baseline design directly answers this question by using logistic regression on the same structured features — demographics, labs, surgery type, surgeon effects — without text. The comparison is clean and the hypothesis is testable.

Stratifying the temporal split by surgeon and complication rate quartile is a sophisticated confound control that addresses the dominant known source of variability in surgical outcomes: individual surgeon skill and case selection. Surgeons have heterogeneous complication rates driven by technique, patient selection, and institutional protocol compliance, and without stratification a temporal split could assign certain surgeons predominantly to training and others to test. The stratified approach ensures that surgical practice variability is balanced across all three splits, converting it from an uncontrolled confound into a measured covariate.

The choice of sensitivity at 85% fixed specificity as the primary metric reflects genuine clinical reasoning. For post-operative complication prediction, the intervention (prophylactic treatment, closer monitoring, earlier discharge criteria adjustment) is low-cost and low-harm relative to a missed complication. The clinical logic favors high recall — capturing as many true complications as possible — at an operationally feasible false positive rate. Setting specificity at 85% fixes the operational constraint and asks how many complications the model can detect within that constraint, which is the relevant clinical question.

The inclusion of surgeon identifier as a fixed effect in the structured feature set is methodologically important. By allowing both models to adjust for surgeon-level baseline complication rates, the comparison tests whether text embeddings add information beyond surgeon identity and structured clinical features — a harder and more honest bar than comparing the neural model to a baseline that cannot account for surgeon-level heterogeneity at all.
all_issues_raised: Comparison confounds multimodal data access and neural capacity: no single-modality neural network condition to decompose the two contributions
Threshold for sensitivity-at-85%-specificity is ambiguous: whether the validation threshold is frozen for test evaluation or recalibrated risks either test set snooping or threshold mismatch
Surgeon fixed-effect feature will have category drift across temporal splits (new/retiring surgeons) that is not addressed
Surgeon stratification within temporal windows may produce degenerate small cells for high-cardinality surgeon IDs
BioBERT-frozen embeddings for preoperative notes may not capture temporal context within notes, and freezing the encoder is not validated as optimal for this domain
all_issues_adjudicated: Comparison confounds multimodal data access and neural capacity: no single-modality neural network condition to decompose the two contributions
Threshold for sensitivity-at-85%-specificity is ambiguous: whether the validation threshold is frozen for test evaluation or recalibrated risks either test set snooping or threshold mismatch
Surgeon fixed-effect feature will have category drift across temporal splits (new/retiring surgeons) that is not addressed
